worth_it_explanation: Supply-chain transparency decides whether a buyer can trace a FreeWheel video or CTV impression back to the publisher who owns it. A sellers.json entry that names the wrong seller relationship, or a SupplyChain object that breaks before it reaches the buyer, makes premium inventory look like resold or unverified supply, which lowers the price advertisers will pay and the demand willing to bid. The check reads records that already exist, the publisher's seller entry on FreeWheel's sellers.json and the schain passed in the bid stream, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete transparency fix the publisher can make.
intro: FreeWheel is a video and Connected TV (CTV) ad server, so the supply it sends into programmatic demand is judged on whether a buyer can verify where each impression came from. Two records carry that proof. The sellers.json file FreeWheel publishes names the seller accounts it transacts on behalf of, and the SupplyChain object (schain) passed in each bid request lists the nodes the impression travels through on its way to the buyer. This subcategory checks that the publisher's own seller entry on FreeWheel's sellers.json is accurate and that the SupplyChain object reaches the buyer complete rather than truncated. These checks confirm the transparency records describe the real selling relationship rather than a stale or incomplete one.

audit_checks.title: The sellers.json entry names the wrong seller ID or relationship
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A buyer reads FreeWheel's sellers.json to verify the entity behind an impression. If the publisher's seller entry carries the wrong seller ID, the wrong relationship of 'PUBLISHER' versus 'INTERMEDIARY', or a confidential flag that hides a relationship the buyer expects to see, the supply reads as unverified or resold, which lowers the price advertisers will pay and can drop demand that filters on a verified chain.

educate.base:
FreeWheel publishes a sellers.json file that lists the seller accounts it transacts on behalf of. A buyer who receives a FreeWheel bid request reads the seller ID in the SupplyChain object, looks that ID up in FreeWheel's sellers.json, and uses the entry to confirm who owns or resells the impression. The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Tech Lab built sellers.json and the SupplyChain object together so a buyer can trace an impression back to the entity that owns the inventory.

Each seller entry carries a few fields that the buyer relies on. The seller_id matches the identifier FreeWheel uses for the account in the bid stream. The seller_type names the relationship, where 'PUBLISHER' means the account is paid directly for inventory it owns, 'INTERMEDIARY' means the account resells inventory it does not own, and 'BOTH' means it does each in different cases. The name and domain identify the seller, unless the entry is marked confidential, which hides those fields from public view. A publisher running owned-and-operated video or Connected TV (CTV) inventory through FreeWheel expects an entry that names them as a 'PUBLISHER' for their seller ID.

The entry goes wrong in a few ways that each cost the publisher transparency. A stale seller ID points to an account FreeWheel no longer maps to the publisher's inventory. A relationship marked 'INTERMEDIARY' on owned inventory tells buyers the supply is resold when it is not. A confidential flag set on a seller that buyers expect to see by name removes the verification a premium CTV buyer looks for. Any of these makes the supply read as something other than what it is.

A few signs point to a seller entry that does not match the real relationship:
  • The seller ID in FreeWheel's sellers.json does not match the ID FreeWheel passes for the publisher in the bid stream
  • The seller_type reads 'INTERMEDIARY' on inventory the publisher owns and operates, or 'PUBLISHER' on inventory it resells
  • The name or domain is missing or marked confidential on a seller that buyers expect to identify by name
You may want to confirm that the publisher's seller entry on FreeWheel's sellers.json names the correct seller ID, the correct relationship, and the expected name and domain. One approach is to pull the seller ID FreeWheel passes in a sample bid request, look it up in the published sellers.json, and check each field against the real selling relationship.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a broadcaster or streaming publisher running owned video and CTV inventory through FreeWheel, the seller entry should read 'PUBLISHER' for inventory the publisher owns because CTV buyers paying premium prices filter heavily on a verified direct relationship. You may want to confirm the entry is not marked 'INTERMEDIARY' or confidential on owned-and-operated channels since either reads to a buyer as resold or unverifiable supply.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher running video around product or how-to content through FreeWheel, the seller entry should still name the publisher as a 'PUBLISHER' for the video inventory it owns, even though video is a smaller part of the property. You may want to confirm the entry reflects that the publisher owns the commerce-content video rather than reselling it, so the supply reads as a verified direct relationship to buyers.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed configuration documentation for seller account setup and sellers.json entries is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the publisher's seller entry in FreeWheel's published sellers.json against the seller ID FreeWheel passes in a sample bid request, and check the relationship, name, and domain against the real selling relationship.

sellers_json_file.label: FreeWheel sellers.json file
steps: Open FreeWheel's published sellers.json file in a browser and search it for the publisher's seller ID.
Read the matched entry and record its seller_id, seller_type ('PUBLISHER', 'INTERMEDIARY', or 'BOTH'), name, domain, and whether the is_confidential flag is set.
Confirm the seller_type matches the real relationship, where owned-and-operated inventory reads 'PUBLISHER' and resold inventory reads 'INTERMEDIARY'.
Note any case where the name or domain is missing or confidential on a seller that buyers are expected to identify by name.

bid_request_inspection.label: Bid request inspection
steps: Capture a sample FreeWheel bid request for the publisher's inventory, using a partner-provided sample or the buyer-facing record if one is available.
Read the seller ID carried in the SupplyChain object for the FreeWheel node.
Compare that seller ID against the seller_id found in FreeWheel's sellers.json, and record whether they match.
Note any mismatch between the ID passed in the bid stream and the ID published in sellers.json because a buyer cannot verify the impression when the two disagree.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the publisher's seller entry from FreeWheel's sellers.json, the seller ID passed in a sample bid request, and the real selling relationship. An entry whose seller ID matches the bid stream, whose seller_type matches the real relationship, and whose name and domain are present as buyers expect passes. An entry with a mismatched seller ID, a relationship that misstates owned versus resold inventory, or a confidential flag that hides a seller buyers expect to identify is a fail recorded with the field that diverges.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the seller entry fields from FreeWheel's sellers.json and the seller ID from a sample bid request above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the seller entry for {{site_name}} in FreeWheel's sellers.json does not match the real selling relationship, which leaves buyers unable to verify the inventory as the publisher intends. A buyer reads FreeWheel's sellers.json to confirm who owns an impression, so a mismatched seller ID, a relationship that reads 'INTERMEDIARY' on owned inventory, or a confidential flag on a seller buyers expect to name all make the supply read as resold or unverified, which lowers the price it can command. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling the published seller entry with the seller ID FreeWheel passes in the bid stream, setting the seller_type to match owned versus resold inventory, and confirming the name and domain are present where buyers expect them. After the change, recheck a sample bid request against the published entry to confirm a buyer can trace the impression back to {{site_name}}.

audit_checks.title: The SupplyChain object is truncated or marked incomplete to the buyer
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The SupplyChain object passed end to end is how a buyer reads every node an impression travels through. If the chain reaches the buyer with the complete flag set to 0, with a missing node, or with a node whose seller ID does not resolve in the matching sellers.json, the buyer cannot trace the supply to its owner, so demand that filters on a complete, verified chain drops out and the price falls.

educate.base:
The SupplyChain object, often called schain, travels with each bid request and lists every entity that handled the impression on its way from the publisher to the buyer. The Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Tech Lab defines it as a chain of nodes, where each node names one entity by its advertising-system identifier (asi) and its seller ID (sid). The object also carries a complete flag, where 1 tells the buyer the chain holds every node back to the owner of the inventory and 0 tells the buyer at least one node is missing.

For FreeWheel video and Connected TV (CTV) inventory, the SupplyChain object reaches the buyer through whatever path the impression takes, which may run directly from FreeWheel or pass through additional resellers first. The buyer reads each node, looks up each seller ID in the matching system's sellers.json, and confirms the chain resolves end to end. A complete, resolvable chain is what lets a premium CTV buyer pay a premium price with confidence about where the impression came from.

The chain breaks the publisher's transparency in a few ways. A complete flag set to 0 tells the buyer the chain is missing a node, so the buyer cannot be sure it sees every party paid. A node dropped along a reseller path leaves a gap the buyer reads as hidden supply. A seller ID in the chain that does not resolve in the named system's sellers.json is a node the buyer cannot verify. Each of these makes the supply look less trustworthy than it is and costs the publisher demand and price.

A few signs point to a SupplyChain object that does not arrive complete and verifiable:
  • The complete flag in the SupplyChain object arrives at the buyer set to 0 rather than 1 for owned, directly sold inventory
  • A reseller path drops or fails to append its node, so the chain the buyer reads is shorter than the real path
  • A seller ID carried in the chain does not resolve to an entry in the matching system's sellers.json
You may want to confirm that the SupplyChain object reaches the buyer complete, with every node present and every seller ID resolvable in the matching sellers.json. One approach is to read the schain on a sample bid request, check the complete flag, and look each node's seller ID up in the sellers.json for the system that node names.

site_type_overlays.publishing: For a broadcaster or streaming publisher, owned CTV inventory sold directly through FreeWheel should produce a SupplyChain object with the complete flag set to 1 and a short, resolvable chain. You may want to confirm the chain is not marked incomplete on directly sold CTV because the buyers paying premium CTV prices are the ones most likely to filter out a chain they cannot fully verify.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a commerce-content publisher, video sold through more than one reseller path can pick up extra nodes before reaching the buyer, and each reseller must append its own node for the chain to stay complete. You may want to trace the schain across each path the commerce-content video takes, so a dropped reseller node does not leave the chain reading as incomplete to the buyer.

educate.learn_more_note: FreeWheel's detailed configuration documentation for how the SupplyChain object is constructed and passed for its inventory is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the SupplyChain object on a sample FreeWheel bid request, check the complete flag, and confirm each node's seller ID resolves in the sellers.json for the system that node names.

bid_request_inspection.label: Bid request inspection
steps: Capture a sample FreeWheel bid request for the publisher's inventory, using a partner-provided sample or the buyer-facing record if one is available.
Read the SupplyChain object and record the value of the complete flag, where 1 means a full chain and 0 means a missing node.
List each node in the chain with its advertising-system identifier (asi) and its seller ID (sid), in the order they appear.
Note whether the chain length matches the real path the impression takes, or whether a reseller node appears to be missing.

sellers_json_lookup.label: sellers.json lookup
steps: For each node in the SupplyChain object, open the sellers.json file for the system named in that node's advertising-system identifier (asi).
Search that file for the node's seller ID (sid) and confirm it resolves to a published entry.
Record any node whose seller ID does not resolve in the matching sellers.json because a buyer reads that node as unverifiable.
Confirm the chain resolves end to end, with every node present and every seller ID found in its matching file.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the complete flag from a sample bid request's SupplyChain object, the nodes in the chain, and whether each node's seller ID resolves in the matching sellers.json. A chain that arrives with the complete flag set to 1, with every node present for the real path, and with every seller ID resolvable passes. A chain marked incomplete, missing a node from the real path, or carrying a seller ID that does not resolve is a fail recorded with the node or flag that breaks the chain.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the complete flag, the chain nodes, and the sellers.json lookup result for each node above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the SupplyChain object for {{site_name}} reaches the buyer incomplete or unverifiable, so a buyer cannot trace the impression to its owner. The SupplyChain object passed end to end is how a buyer reads every node an impression travels through, so a complete flag set to 0, a dropped reseller node, or a seller ID that does not resolve in the matching sellers.json all read as hidden or unverified supply and cost the inventory demand and price. {{voice_recommend}} tracing the schain on a sample bid request, confirming the complete flag reads 1 for directly sold inventory, and resolving every node's seller ID in the sellers.json for the system that node names. After the change, recheck a sample bid request to confirm the chain arrives complete and every node resolves back to {{site_name}}.
